peer_exemplars.reasoning: High agreeableness and trust in institutions, combined with very high authoritarian submission and conventionalism, support moderate backing for transparency as a way to maintain order and trust, but low openness and libertarian anti-censorship orientation temper enthusiasm for heavy-handed mandates.
High openness and internal political efficacy drive support for transparency, but low trust in institutions and moderate libertarian anti-censorship views temper enthusiasm to a mild level.
High openness and GAL-TAN values drive strong support for transparency, tempered by low conscientiousness and moderate trust in institutions.

High extraversion and low agreeableness (HEXACO) combined with strong anti-elitism and distrust of institutions (trust in science at 2.57) produce moderate support for transparency as a check on opaque power, but low trust in government (7.77) tempers enthusiasm for mandated disclosure.

High libertarian anti-censorship orientation (68.92) and skepticism of state control (74.04) create moderate support for transparency as a check on platform power, but very low agreeableness (0.61) and high authoritarian law-and-order preference (81.36) temper enthusiasm for mandated disclosure.

While my strong care/harm foundation and moderate anti-censorship lean support transparency to prevent harm, my deeply traditional-authoritarian worldview and very low openness make me skeptical of forcing private platforms to reveal proprietary systems, resulting in mild support.

Very high openness, high agreeableness, and very strong GAL-TAN green/libertarian values produce firm support for transparency to protect against manipulation, though high authoritarianism slightly tempers the confidence in bottom-up enforcement.

High conscientiousness and very low openness to experience combine with strong authoritarian law-and-order preferences to produce moderate support for mandated transparency as a rule-based accountability measure, though low libertarian anti-censorship orientation tempers enthusiasm.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: High authoritarian submission and conventionalism, combined with high trust in institutions and strong authority/subversion moral foundation, drive moderate-to-strong support for regulating platforms to enforce accountability, though low openness and moderate libertarian skepticism prevent maximal support.

Moderate authoritarian submission and a dangerous worldview lean toward regulation, but low trust in institutions, moderate libertarian skepticism of state control, and a dismissive-avoidant style produce near-neutral mild opposition.
Moderate support due to high trust in institutions but balanced by libertarian skepticism of regulation.

Low trust in government (7.77) and high anti-elitism (52.77) combined with a strong free spirit (67.34) and disruptor (68.94) user type produce mild opposition to regulation, seen as potentially stifling innovation and freedom.

High libertarian skepticism of state control (74.04) and very low agreeableness (0.61) drive opposition to regulation, though high authoritarian law-and-order (81.36) and punitive attitudes (72.85) create some internal tension.
My moderate libertarian skepticism of state control and market-oriented economic views lead to mild opposition despite my support for order and fairness.
Care/harm and fairness motives strongly favor regulation, but libertarian skepticism of state control and some economic policy conservatism pull the score down from a higher level.
High authoritarian acceptance of coercive social control and low libertarian skepticism of state control, combined with high conscientiousness, produce moderate support for regulation.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Very high traditional authoritarian nationalism (98.8%), civic nationalism (98.8%), and trust in government (69.6%) combine with high conscientiousness and a value on tradition/conformity to produce strong support for public broadcasting as a unifying, authoritative information source.
Moderate economic conservatism on public sector role and low trust in government lead to mild opposition, despite high openness to diverse content and a philanthropic orientation.
High trust in institutions and GAL-TAN values strongly support public broadcasting, despite low authoritarianism.

Extremely low trust in government (7.77) and science (2.57) combined with high anti-elitism (52.77) and a strong competitive worldview (87.64) generate clear opposition to funding a state-backed media entity perceived as an elite institution.

Low trust in government (34.96) and high libertarian anti-censorship (68.92) oppose state-funded media, reinforced by very low agreeableness (0.61) and high ethnic nationalism (91.44) that may view public broadcasting as elite-driven.
Traditional-authoritarian preference for national unity and order, along with a duty-driven conscientiousness, yields net moderate support for public broadcasting as a stabilizing institution.
Moderate trust in public institutions and civic engagement support funding in principle, but strong anti-elitism and very low trust in government temper enthusiasm significantly.
Moderate trust in mainstream journalism and high GAL values are offset by low openness to experience and a dismissive-avoidant attachment style, yielding near-neutral ambivalence.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Low openness to experience and high authoritarian submission create mild opposition to giving users choice over algorithms, seen as potentially undermining social order and exposing people to harmful content, despite moderate digital literacy.
High openness and a free-spirit HEXAD profile support user autonomy in algorithmic choice, while moderate libertarian anti-censorship aligns with this, yielding clear support.
Moderate support from high openness and autonomy values, but low conscientiousness may limit perceived need for mandates.

High extraversion (98.32) and free spirit autonomy (67.25) combined with moderate openness (57.59) produce mild support for giving users control over algorithms, though low trust in regulators to implement this effectively limits stronger endorsement.

High libertarian anti-censorship (68.92) and free spirit exploration (76.39) support user choice, but low openness to experience (22.69) and high authoritarian conformity (67.35) reduce enthusiasm for system-level changes.
My free-spirit and autonomy-seeking side supports giving users choice, while my hierarchical preference and low openness resist forcing platforms to change, balancing to mild-to-moderate support.
Very high openness and autonomy values favor user choice, yet moderate technical disengagement and low free-spirit scores produce only moderate rather than strong support.
High HEXACO openness to experience and free-spirit autonomy support choice, but low libertarian anti-censorship orientation and high authoritarianism keep support mild.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: High moral foundations for sanctity/purity (84.8%) and authority/subversion (99.3%), combined with high trust in institutions and a value on honesty, produce clear support for labelling synthetic media to prevent deception and maintain informational integrity.
A dangerous worldview and high conscientiousness (order) strongly favor labeling to prevent deception, while high openness to new tech makes the mandate seem practical, resulting in clear support.
High openness and care/harm foundation strongly support labeling to prevent harm, tempered by low authoritarianism.

High conscientiousness (70.56) and a dangerous worldview (71.59) combined with strong care/harm foundation (93.03) drive moderate support for labelling synthetic media as a protective measure, though libertarian anti-censorship leanings (57.37) prevent stronger support.

Strong care/harm foundation (76.34) and high harm avoidance (95.76) support labeling to prevent deception, reinforced by high authoritarian acceptance of coercive social control (85.23) and moderate trust in science (56.75).
Strong sanctity/purity concerns and a desire to protect informational order drive clear support for labelling synthetic media, moderated slightly by my anti-censorship bent.
Strong honesty-humility and integrity values combine with high care/harm to support labeling to prevent deception, although low trust in enforcement institutions prevents a higher score.
Very high neuroticism and threat sensitivity near zero, combined with high conscientiousness and strong support for rule-based order, produce clear support for mandatory labelling.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: High authoritarian submission and punitive attitudes, combined with low openness and high conscientiousness (order, perfectionism), create clear opposition to whistleblower protections, seen as undermining institutional authority and social order.

High internal political efficacy and a philanthropic drive for knowledge sharing strongly support protections, while moderate libertarian anti-surveillance and low trust in institutions add caution, yielding clear support.
High openness, GAL-TAN values, and fairness foundation strongly support protections for researchers.

High extraversion (98.32) and low agreeableness (HEXACO 2.74) combined with strong anti-elitism (52.77) and a disruptor user type (68.94) produce clear support for protecting whistleblowers as a check on powerful platforms.

High libertarian anti-domination (90.15) and resistance to bullying/tyranny (95.39) strongly support whistleblower protections, while high authoritarian law-and-order (81.36) creates some hesitation about destabilizing platforms.
My strong care/harm and fairness impulses support protecting truth-tellers, but high respect for authority and order create hesitation, yielding moderate support.

Very high internal political efficacy, strong fairness/justice values, and high civic engagement align to protect insiders who expose wrongdoing, though moderate authoritarian submission slightly restrains enthusiasm.
High moral foundation of fairness/equality and high GAL civil liberties orientation, combined with moderate trust in science, drive clear support for protections.
